findings.title: Never pause acquisition — run it as a 24/7 autopilot system
findings.claim: The single biggest lever that broke Ellis past $30K/month was an acquisition system that never stops, not better marketing or chatting.
findings.what_they_do: Ellis runs a three-component acquisition machine (lead-magnet content + paid ads + a specific outreach method) combined with VSLs, a funnel, a call-setting service, and a closer — all running simultaneously and never paused even when onboarding a new creator. The system converts models continuously without founder involvement.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and effectively pauses when attention shifts to delivery (§6). There is no documented funnel, VSL, or call-setting layer, and acquisition is not yet automated or delegated.
findings.recommendation: Document Palm's current outreach method into an SOP, then hire/delegate outreach execution so acquisition runs independently of delivery. Add a funnel layer (lead-magnet video → VSL → booked call) to qualify higher-tier creators before the call. Treat acquisition as an always-on background process, not a sequential phase.

sources.quote: the only thing that took us past it, it wasn't marketing, it wasn't chatting, it was getting a solid acquisition system that never stops running

findings.title: Stop signing low-tier creators — quality floor determines revenue ceiling
findings.claim: Low-tier models produce low-tier results regardless of agency skill, so raising the minimum quality bar of creators signed is a prerequisite to scaling past $50K/month.
findings.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is outbound-DM-led with no documented quality-scoring rubric for creator tiers (§6). There is no lead-magnet content funnel that pre-qualifies creator quality before outreach.
findings.recommendation: Define a minimum creator quality rubric (work ethic signals, existing audience, content output history) and apply it as a gate before onboarding. Produce story-driven case-study content (process videos, not just stat screenshots) to attract self-selecting high-tier creators inbound.

sources.quote: if you keep acquiring low tier models, you're going to get low tier results because you got low tier work ethic and it's not going to scale

findings.title: Pick one traffic platform and become the agency's 'guru' of it before adding others
findings.claim: Trying to master multiple platforms simultaneously dilutes bandwidth and produces mastery of none; Ellis's agency specialises exclusively in Instagram as the highest-converting platform for OF.
findings.what_they_do: Ellis's team picks Instagram as the primary platform because it supports clickable bio links, daily story CTAs, and multi-format content (video, photo, carousel). Content is then repurposed to TikTok/YouTube/Snapchat secondarily, but the team's deep algorithm knowledge stays on IG. Each creator is then assigned specific content verticals within that platform, tested over ~6 weeks, and viral ingredients extracted from data.
findings.vs_us: Palm's posting system covers IG and TikTok via Publer/Telegram (§5) but the traffic strategy and platform prioritisation is not documented (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm's content pipeline is data-driven on the ideation side (§1) but the 6-week test-and-extract cadence per creator is not formalised.
findings.recommendation: Formally designate Instagram as Palm's primary traffic platform in the ops baseline. Document a 6-week content vertical testing cadence per creator: assign 2–3 verticals, post consistently, pull performance data, double down on viral ingredients. Repurpose IG content to TikTok/Snapchat without splitting strategic focus.

sources.quote: Instagram, in my opinion, is the most powerful platform. So, once you figure out what you're going to master, become the fucking guru of that platform

findings.title: Assign each creator 2–3 content verticals matched to her strengths, then iterate on viral data
findings.claim: Locking a creator into specific content verticals aligned with her strengths makes results predictable and gives the agency a repeatable creative direction model.
findings.what_they_do: For each creator, Ellis identifies her natural strengths and interests, maps them to 2–3 content verticals (e.g. cooking niche with cooking outfits), produces content across those verticals for ~6 weeks, analyses performance data, extracts the viral ingredients, and scales what works. The creator's brief is narrow enough that ideas are abundant and filming is sustainable.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline scrapes viral inspo and promotes reels to a creator's board (§1), which is data-driven but inspo-led rather than creator-vertical-led. There is no documented step where a creator is formally assigned 2–3 fixed verticals as a strategic frame before ideation begins.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'vertical assignment' step at creator onboarding: define 2–3 content verticals per creator based on her strengths/interests, then filter inspo scraping (§1 pipeline) to those verticals first. After 6 weeks of posting, run a formal data review to codify which verticals are outperforming, and adjust the inspo source list accordingly.

sources.quote: once you've found the content verticals, it's about trying to turn the creator into the best creator within that space — results then become predictable

findings.title: Systemise the OF backend before scaling traffic — uncontrolled sub inflow breaks unprepared teams
findings.claim: An unsystemised OF backend (no tracking links, no per-creator content plan, inconsistent handling) will collapse under sudden high sub volume, wasting the traffic wins.
findings.what_they_do: Ellis describes an incident where a creator brought in 3,500 subs overnight and the agency had to pull chatters from other models, scramble chat managers, and operate chaotically. His prescribed fix: implement tracking links per traffic source, standardise how every creator's page is managed (content plan, PPV plan, data review), and ensure every process has an SOP so any new chatter/manager can be slotted in without handholding.
findings.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV backend is THIN (§2) with no documented standardisation of per-creator page management or tracking links. Chat is outsourced (§8) which partially insulates Palm from the chaos scenario, but the lack of standardised backend setup means scaling a creator's traffic still risks operational breakdown.
findings.recommendation: Before aggressively scaling any creator's traffic, complete a backend readiness checklist: (1) tracking links live per platform, (2) PPV content plan drafted, (3) chatter capacity confirmed with external team, (4) escalation SOP defined. This is also foundational groundwork for the in-house chat roadmap (§8 build-toward).

sources.quote: you have no tracking mechanism — you're probably not using tracking links to see where the traffic's coming from, and every single creator is getting handled in a different way

findings.title: Write the SOP before hiring, not after — every role must have a documented replacement path
findings.claim: Without an SOP for every role, a new hire requires weeks of founder handholding, negating the time savings of hiring and blocking agency growth.
findings.what_they_do: Ellis and his partner documented every job into SOPs before delegating. They reinvested 100% of revenue for the first 12 months into the next hire, next system, or software — taking no personal salary. The founder's job is specifically to build the SOP so the hire can self-onboard and replace the founder in that function.
findings.vs_us: Palm's team/org structure is THIN (§10) with no documented org chart, hiring scorecards, or comp bands. Josh currently handles acquisition personally (§6), which Ellis explicitly identifies as a founder bottleneck to eliminate first.
findings.recommendation: Prioritise SOP creation for the two highest-founder-time functions (acquisition outreach and creator onboarding). Write the SOP while doing the job, then hire against it. Model Ellis's reinvestment discipline: define a minimum reinvestment rate of revenue back into team/systems before founder draw, especially during the $30K–$100K scaling phase.

sources.quote: if you do not have an SOP for every single part of your business, every single job, every single role, then again, you're never going to make it

findings.title: Train chatters to exceed founder-level performance rather than chatting yourself
findings.claim: Founders who chat themselves get short-term quality gains but sacrifice the bandwidth needed to get results, attract better creators, and scale.
findings.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly stops chatting himself and redirects that time into training chatters to surpass his own skill level. He frames chatter training as a higher-leverage use of founder time than doing the chatting, because it unblocks bandwidth for content direction, acquisition, and results that attract higher-tier creators.
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting to an external team today (§8) — this is already structurally aligned with Ellis's recommendation. The build-toward for Palm is in-house chat team training and AI chatting (§8 roadmap). Ellis's training-focus framing is directly applicable to designing the in-house chatter onboarding and quality-ramp process.
findings.recommendation: As Palm builds toward an in-house chat team (§8 roadmap), design the programme around a 'train chatters to beat founder level' benchmark rather than a floor-level compliance model. Document what a 10/10 chat interaction looks like, build that into chatter SOPs and QA scoring, and assign a chat manager whose sole job is raising chatter skill — not just monitoring volume.

sources.quote: if you are chatting yourself, you're not going to have that bandwidth — the key there is to train your chatters to become better than you

findings.topic: Content production guidelines for scripted DM sessions
findings.title: Film PPV script content with no sunlight, consistent location/outfit/makeup to simulate 'live' feel
findings.claim: Scripted PPV content must be filmed to appear live — no natural light, same clothes/makeup/location throughout — so chatters can convincingly sell it as happening in real-time.
findings.what_they_do: They enforce four production rules before filming any DM script content: (1) block all natural light so the session can be 'sold' as nighttime at any hour, (2) same location every clip, (3) same outfit throughout, (4) same makeup. The goal is verisimilitude — the fan should believe the model is filming live for him specifically.
findings.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV content system is THIN (§2) and DM monetization is outsourced (§8). There is no internal SOP governing how scripted PPV content is filmed for use in DM sessions. This is a foundational gap for the in-house chat roadmap.
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, create a 'script content filming brief' that mandates these four consistency rules. Add to the OF vault content spec (§2) as a distinct content type: 'DM script sets' with a checklist covering lighting, wardrobe continuity, location, and makeup lock-in before any shoot begins.

sources.quote: no sunlight. So, when the content is being filmed, there should be no natural light coming in
same location, same clothes, and same makeup. The most important thing over here is to make the fan believe that the script is actually filmed live

findings.title: Pre-shoot 'verification pictures' (peace sign, spoon, etc.) to neutralize fan skepticism during sessions
findings.claim: Having a library of pre-shot 'random gesture' verification photos allows chatters to instantly defuse fans who doubt the content is being filmed live for them.
findings.what_they_do: Before any DM session, the model shoots a bank of verification-style photos — holding a peace sign, thumbs up, spoon on head, etc. When a fan challenges authenticity ('I don't believe you're doing this live'), the chatter sends one of these photos on demand. Because the images look spontaneous and fan-requestable, they eliminate doubt without the model needing to actually film anything new.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline for this. Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented asset library concept for DM sessions. This is a build-toward gap for the in-house chat roadmap.
findings.recommendation: Add 'verification shot bank' as a mandatory deliverable in the OF vault/content brief for any creator whose DM sessions will use a scripted approach. Define minimum: 5–10 varied gesture photos, shot in the same session as the script content (consistent lighting/wardrobe). Document this in the future in-house chat SOP.

sources.quote: you need verification pictures. So to any pictures, this is just basically like holding a peace sign, thumbs up, maybe a spoon on your head
you can just send a picture like this or he will even maybe request like hold a piece and hold a spoon on your head and you have them ready to eliminate that thought

findings.topic: DM session structure and rapport phase
findings.title: Open every DM session with 5–20 message rapport phase before any sexual pivot
findings.claim: Every DM session — whether with a new or returning subscriber — should open with a non-sexual rapport phase to qualify the fan and create emotional investment before monetization begins.
findings.what_they_do: New subscribers: 5–20 messages gathering personal info (job, hobbies) with light flirting, zero sexual content. Returning subscribers: brief small talk, ask about their day, deploy inside jokes if available. Only after this rapport block does the chatter pivot toward sexting. The report section is explicitly never scripted — it must feel genuine — but everything after it is.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8); no internal rapport SOP exists. This is a high-signal build-toward finding. The distinction between scripted vs. unscripted phases is particularly useful for designing chatter training.
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, encode a mandatory unscripted rapport phase at session open. Define minimum message count by subscriber type (new: ~10 msgs; returning: ~5 msgs). Train chatters that this phase is intentionally freeform — the goal is genuine connection data that feeds downstream personalization. Document inside-joke capture as a CRM field (Palm already has Fan Tracker §9 — add an 'inside jokes / personal details' field per fan).

sources.quote: First thing you want to do when a fan comes to the page is build report. This is five to 20 messages depending on the fan and the communication
The report part is never scripted because it has to feel genuine, but everything after that is scripted

findings.title: Use passive teaser content (towel/shower clips) to make the fan initiate sexual escalation, not the chatter
findings.claim: Chatters should never directly initiate sexual conversation — instead, send an ambiguous teaser that makes sexual escalation the fan's idea, reducing friction and preserving the illusion of authenticity.
findings.what_they_do: Chatter sends a low-reveal teaser (e.g., model in a towel with wet hair, caption: 'just got out of the shower') with no sexual framing. The fan reads the signal and responds sexually — he has technically initiated. Alternatively, a 3-second clip of the model waving to camera with a self-deprecating caption ('my face looks so bad right now') achieves the same passive initiation. Once the fan escalates, the chatter follows his lead and layers in a second, slightly more revealing teaser before setting up the first PPV.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8 outsourced). This is a high-value build-toward mechanic — it maps directly to the psychological structure Palm will need when scripting AI chatting (active roadmap project, §8).
findings.recommendation: In the in-house/AI chat SOP, codify 'fan-initiated escalation' as a required step between rapport and first PPV. Build a library of passive teaser content categories (shower, waving, casual home setting) and matching neutral captions. For AI chatting specifically, this mechanic is low-risk and high-leverage — the AI never has to make a sexual first move.

sources.quote: the most important part here is to let him initiate a sexual conversation
you have a teaser where the model is in a towel with wet hair and you can just say just got out of the shower. Now you didn't say anything sexual here

findings.topic: PPV ladder and first-PPV price anchoring
findings.title: Price first PPV at $15–$25 and use a 'Yes Train' commitment loop to pre-sell before sending
findings.claim: A soft verbal commitment ('yes train') before sending the first PPV — combined with a $15–$25 price point and partial-nudity content — significantly increases buy-through rates.
findings.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter runs a 'yes train': a question that sounds like consent ('if I do this, do you promise you'll go easy on me?') but functions as a purchase pre-commitment. When the fan says yes, he feels psychologically obligated to buy the PPV that follows. First PPV is priced $15–$25 (higher if the model is positioned as premium; lower if marketed with explicit free content). Content is partial nudity only (top off). Subsequent PPVs escalate: bottoms off → full nude → solo play. Between each PPV, the chatter rebuilds arousal with free teasers and descriptive text before the next ask. The session ends when the fan signals spending fatigue — at which point no further PPV is pushed.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or ladder (§7 GAP — noted as '#1 creator concern'). This is a concrete, tested pricing structure from a $100k/month operation. The 'yes train' pre-sell mechanic is also absent from any internal SOP.
findings.recommendation: Adopt this as a starting-point PPV ladder for the in-house chat team: first PPV $15–$25 (partial nudity), second PPV (full nudity) $20–$35, third+ (solo play) $25–$50+. Adjust per creator tier. Document the 'yes train' as a mandatory pre-PPV step in the chat SOP. Also use this ladder to inform the pricing section (§7) — this is the most concrete competitor data Palm has on PPV structure.

sources.quote: the yes train is basically getting a confirmation, getting a yes from him so that when later you send the PPV, he feels obliged to buy it because he already said yes
first PPV you will price $15 to $25 this depends on your model where you market her and how you market her

findings.title: Send a 'gratification video' at session end to convert spent fans into loyal returning subscribers
findings.claim: Sending a warm, smiling 'thank you' video after a fan's spending session — when he is emotionally most vulnerable — builds the relationship bond that drives repeat monetization rather than churn.
findings.what_they_do: When a fan signals spending fatigue, the chatter stops pushing PPVs entirely. Instead, the model sends a pre-filmed 'gratification video': short clip of her smiling, saying she had a great time, expressing genuine-seeming warmth. The chatter then attempts to extend the chat slightly rather than letting the fan leave immediately. The logic: the fan is emotionally open at this moment; a positive close imprints a good memory that makes him return for the full PPV ladder again next session.
findings.vs_us: Palm has partial fan retention infrastructure (whale hunting, Fan Tracker — §9) but no documented post-session strategy or churn prevention tactic at the individual fan level. This is a concrete retention mechanic that complements the existing Fan Tracker CRM.
findings.recommendation: Add 'gratification close' as a required final step in the in-house chat SOP. Pre-film one gratification video per creator per content set. In the Fan Tracker (§9), add a 'last gratification sent' timestamp field to track recency and ensure it's deployed consistently. Consider this the retention equivalent of a post-purchase email — systematize it the same way.

sources.quote: this is just a simple video of the model saying thank you and that she had a really good time to the camera being happy smiling. So you make the fan feel good. This is where they're most vulnerable to relationship building
you repeat this process until the fan can't spend anymore. When the fan can't spend anymore, you don't want to force any more sales because this will just ruin the whole relationship

findings.title: Build DM scripts empirically: test freestyle lines, promote high-performing ones into the canonical script
findings.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
findings.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and no internal script system exists. For the AI chatting roadmap project specifically, this empirical methodology is directly applicable — Palm could A/B test AI chat lines against fan response data and auto-promote winning variants. This also aligns with Palm's existing data-driven content ideation approach (§1).
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team and AI chatting system, implement a structured script iteration process: (1) define the structural framework as non-negotiable, (2) allow freestyle within phases, (3) require chatters to flag high-performing lines with a simple tagging system (Airtable or Telegram), (4) conduct weekly script review to promote winners. For AI chatting, this maps to prompt versioning with conversion tracking — build that feedback loop into the architecture from day one.

sources.quote: If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works

findings.title: Replace generic greetings with open-ended, personalized openers
findings.claim: Using open-ended, flirty questions instead of generic greetings like 'hey' or 'how are you' dramatically increases subscriber engagement.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to never open with 'hello' or 'how are you'; instead they ask about the fan's day in a flirty, playful way, reference previous conversations ('you made me blush'), and use the subscriber's name periodically to create intimacy. Example opener: 'Hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush.'
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is currently outsourced to an external chat team (§8); no internal SOP or scripted opener standards exist. This is directly relevant build-toward material for the in-house chat team roadmap.
findings.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat SOP, codify a banned-phrases list ('hey', 'how are you', 'okay') and a required-opener format: fan name + callback to prior interaction + flirty open-ended question. Embed this in chatter onboarding scripts.

sources.quote: use open-ended questions instead of just typing hello or greetings ask engaging questions avoid asking how or you because it is so boring
instead of copy pasting a generic hey try messaging hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush

findings.title: Send blurred/cropped preview before full PPV to spark curiosity and drive unlock
findings.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
findings.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV sequencing SOP (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is a concrete mechanical step missing from any documented Palm chatting or PPV workflow.
findings.recommendation: Adopt the blur/crop preview as a standard step in the PPV send sequence for both the outsourced chat team (share as guidance) and future in-house SOP. Editors/content team should produce a 'teaser variant' of each PPV asset at upload time to make this operationally easy.

sources.quote: use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity

findings.topic: Urgency and exclusivity framing for PPV
findings.title: Apply time-sensitive + VIP-exclusivity framing to every PPV offer
findings.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
findings.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.
findings.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing or sales-framing baseline exists at Palm (§7 GAP). The external chat team may or may not use these frames; Palm has no oversight of that language.
findings.recommendation: Include both urgency and exclusivity language templates in the PPV script library Palm will build for its in-house chat team. Also share these frames with the current outsourced team as a performance-improvement brief. Track conversion rate before/after to validate.

sources.quote: make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you

findings.title: Offer content bundles to increase average order value over single-item PPV
findings.claim: Packaging multiple pieces of content together at a 'special price' increases perceived value and raises average transaction size.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters pitch bundles during PPV conversations: e.g., 'get three exclusive pics plus a surprise video for a special price.' The bundle is presented as a deal, not a push, and is integrated naturally into the flow of conversation rather than sent as a standalone promo.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or bundle structure (§7 GAP). Single-item PPV is presumably the default through the outsourced team.
findings.recommendation: Design a tiered PPV menu: individual asset price, 3-item bundle at ~20% discount, and a monthly 'vault' bundle. Give the chat team (outsourced now, in-house later) explicit bundle scripts and price points so upselling is consistent across accounts.

sources.quote: offer bundles and packages people are more likely to buy if they feel like they're getting a better deal

findings.title: Triage multi-account chat queues by subscriber spend history, not recency
findings.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a Whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but no documented protocol for chat prioritization within the outsourced team. This is a logical extension of the whale-hunting data Palm already collects.
findings.recommendation: Export whale/high-spend fan flags from Palm's Fan Tracker into whatever interface the chat team uses, and formalize a 'respond to these first' rule. This bridges the existing whale-hunting infrastructure (§9) with chat execution — a quick operational win.

sources.quote: prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

findings.title: Chatters maintain per-fan notes (age, relationship status, interests) to personalize every session
findings.claim: Chatters who record personal details shared by fans during conversations can use those details in future sessions to create a sense of genuine ongoing relationship.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to jot down fan-disclosed details — name, age, relationship status, hobbies, personal anecdotes — and reference them in later messages. The explicit goal is to make the fan feel 'seen, heard, and valued' rather than like one of many subscribers.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but it is oriented toward spend/whale detection rather than personal-detail logging for chat personalization. The outsourced team's note-taking practices are unknown.
findings.recommendation: Extend Fan Tracker with a free-text 'fan notes' field (personal details, interests, shared anecdotes) that chatters can read before and write to after each session. This aligns with Palm's existing CRM infrastructure and directly improves re-engagement quality.

sources.quote: jot down details about them you want to know more than just their name keep track of things like their age relationship status and any personal tibbits they share

findings.title: Track which message templates drive the most sales and circulate winners across the team
findings.claim: Systematically monitoring which specific messages generate the most engagement and sales, then sharing winners as team templates, compounds chatter performance over time.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to set daily sales targets, track which message styles and scripts convert best, and collaborate with teammates to share high-performing templates. If a flirty line is working across multiple fans, it gets codified as a reusable template for the whole team.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat performance tracking or template iteration loop (§8 OUTSOURCED). The outsourced team is a black box for this kind of feedback. Palm's content pipeline (§1) does this well for social content (viral scoring, promote-best) but the same logic has never been applied to chat.
findings.recommendation: Apply Palm's existing 'scrape → analyze → promote best → reuse' logic from the content pipeline (§1) to chat scripts: have the outsourced team (and eventually in-house) log top-converting messages weekly, review them in a shared doc, and build a living script library. This is a direct structural parallel to a system Palm already operates well.

sources.quote: if a certain flirty message is working well for multiple subscribers you can use it as a template for future chats
track your metrics monitor which messages and strategies generate the most engagement in sales

findings.topic: Payment collection method — bank swap vs invoice
findings.title: Two-method payment collection: bank-info swap vs end-of-period invoice
findings.claim: Agencies can either replace the model's OnlyFans bank details with their own (leaving the model's name intact) or simply invoice the model for their commission percentage after the model withdraws earnings.
findings.what_they_do: Method 1: Agency swaps the bank account number on the creator's OF account to the agency's account, leaving the account name unchanged so OF doesn't flag it; agency receives all revenue, then pays creator their cut. Method 2: Agency leaves bank info as-is, creator withdraws at month/week end, agency sends a formal invoice (date, agreed split, amount owed, payment deadline) and creator pays the agency's cut separately. Bjorn says he now uses Method 2 for nearly all models and is migrating to 100% invoicing.
findings.vs_us: Palm already uses invoicing (§7): weekly auto-computed PDFs via Puppeteer, emailed via Resend, with commission % set at onboarding. Method 1 (bank swap) is not part of Palm's documented approach. Palm's invoicing is weekly; Bjorn describes monthly or weekly invoicing as a choice.
findings.recommendation: Palm's existing weekly invoicing aligns with Bjorn's preferred Method 2 — no change needed on the fundamental approach. However, Palm should explicitly document WHY it uses invoicing (trust signal to creators, easier onboarding, avoids virtual-bank bans) as part of creator pitch/onboarding materials so managers can articulate it confidently during signing conversations.

sources.quote: at the end of the month you send your invoice to the model saying here's everything your account is earned for the month then here's the percentage we graded on minus that

findings.topic: Bank-swap mechanics — virtual vs brick-and-mortar banks
findings.title: Bank-swap only works reliably with brick-and-mortar banks; avoid Wise
findings.claim: Swapping OnlyFans bank details to the agency's account works with traditional brick-and-mortar banks even if the account name differs, but most virtual/neobanks (especially Wise) will reject it or ban the account.
findings.what_they_do: Bjorn tested Wise for this use case and was banned — Wise requested business verification, then gave him 48 hours to withdraw funds before permanent ban. He found that physical/legacy banks accept name-account mismatches and funds land correctly. He explicitly warns against Wise for this purpose.
findings.vs_us: Palm uses invoicing rather than bank swaps, so this is largely moot for current operations. However, Palm has no documented banking/payment-processor stack for agency-side receiving accounts — this is a §7 gap. The Wise ban risk is relevant if Palm ever experiments with Method 1 or uses Wise for any agency treasury function.
findings.recommendation: Log as a cautionary data point: do not use Wise for any OF payment-flow adjacency. If Palm ever evaluates Method 1 for specific creator relationships (e.g., high-risk retention situations), restrict to traditional bank accounts only. More immediately, ensure Palm's own agency receiving accounts are on stable, business-friendly banking rails (not neobanks with aggressive KYB).

sources.quote: don't use wise for that they're dogs I got banned for doing that

findings.topic: Bank-swap as a hard sell — trust dynamics with creators
findings.title: Asking creators to swap bank info is a high-friction pitch; invoicing reduces creator resistance
findings.claim: Requesting a bank-info swap is a 'hard sell' that spooks creators, especially early in the relationship, whereas invoicing leaves the creator in control and removes that friction entirely.
findings.what_they_do: Bjorn notes that bank swaps require the creator to actively approve an email verification step, creating a moment of distrust. He frames invoicing as the trust-building alternative: the model never feels she's lost control of her money, and the agency relies on relationship quality (delivering results) rather than financial lock-in to ensure payment.
findings.vs_us: Palm's invoicing model (§7) already eliminates this friction. However, Palm has no documented creator-pitch script addressing payment mechanics — this is a §6/§7 gap. Creators may still have anxiety about payment flows even with invoicing if it's not explained upfront.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'how we get paid' explainer to Palm's creator onboarding wizard or pitch deck: 'You keep control of your account and withdraw as normal; we send you a weekly invoice for our agreed commission.' This proactively neutralizes a common creator objection and positions Palm as the trustworthy, professional option vs. agencies that demand bank swaps.

sources.quote: It's A Hard Sell so if you haven't been doing this for a while you're on your first model or you haven't really worked with the model for very long this is going to be a hard sale

findings.title: Performance-based confidence: good results eliminate creator flight risk better than financial lock-in
findings.claim: If an agency is doing a genuinely good job for a creator, the creator won't withhold payment or leave, because abandoning the agency mid-month costs her more money than she'd gain.
findings.what_they_do: Bjorn's argument: the agency's leverage isn't financial control (bank swap) but value delivery. A creator who is earning significantly more with the agency than she would alone won't risk that income by stiffing the agency. He uses this logic to justify switching all clients to invoicing — the quality of results is the retention mechanism.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented retention or performance-success cadence for creators (§9 gap). The implicit logic matches — Palm presumably aims to deliver results — but there's no written framework for measuring 'are we doing a good enough job that this creator won't leave?' or communicating that value back to the creator.
findings.recommendation: Develop a simple creator-facing performance report (monthly or weekly): earnings this period, earnings trend, key milestones. Sending this proactively before invoicing reinforces the value Palm delivers and makes the invoice feel deserved. This directly supports payment security without any financial lock-in mechanics. Ties into §9 retention gap.

sources.quote: if you are that worried then you are doing a shitty job because if you were doing a really good job for the model then you can have confidence that they're not going to go anywhere

findings.title: Invoice weekly rather than monthly to reduce exposure to creator non-payment
findings.claim: Agencies worried about non-payment should invoice weekly rather than monthly, reducing the maximum amount at risk at any given time.
findings.what_they_do: Bjorn mentions weekly invoicing as a practical risk-mitigation option for agencies that are anxious about creators running with earnings. He frames it as a simple operational adjustment rather than a trust-control mechanism, keeping the relationship dynamic healthy while capping downside.
findings.vs_us: Palm already invoices weekly (§7) — this is already our practice. Palm is ahead here.
findings.recommendation: No change needed — Palm's weekly invoicing cadence already implements this best practice. Document this explicitly in creator onboarding materials as a feature ('you get clear weekly accounting') rather than leaving it implicit.
